data.title: Navios Maritime Partners Remains A Buy After A 100% Rally
data.description:
I’ve covered Navios Maritime Partners (NMM) before. In my initial article I outlined why I considered it a strong investment and a low risk opportunity; since then, the stock has returned more than 100%. Q4 results were great and above analysts’ estimates, and I expect a similar outcome in Q1. This, together with the steady repurchases, still makes it an interesting buy.

Background

Navios is the largest listed diversified shipping company by number of vessels and second largest by DWT with exposure across drybulk, containers, and tankers.

The company has continued to actively manage its fleet, as mentioned in my past articles. Navios has taken advantage of the elevated second hand VLCC prices to divest two of them generating $136.5 million in gross proceeds. This reinforces management’s strategy of selling older tonnage and reinvesting into newbuilds. At the same time, Navios now has 26 newbuild vessels following the delivery of an LR2 tanker and it has continued ordering new vessels with two additional Capesize vessels with delivery scheduled for 2028 and 2029. Following Navios strategy, these vessels have already been chartered out for five years with a floor rate of $25,000 per day plus a 50% profit sharing component, securing downside protection while maintaining upside exposure.

This is a key characteristic of Navios. While the fleet is balanced across these segments, the company’s earnings continue to be mainly driven by drybulk, as containership vessels are on long term fixed contracts and tankers have only limited index linked days. Most of the linked index or spot exposure is in the drybulk segment, which provides most of the volatility in quarterly earnings.

This approach, combined with the company’s contract structure, has resulted in a backlog of approximately $3.8 billion providing strong visibility for the coming years.

The company’s capital allocation strategy continues to prioritize deleveraging first until it reaches a net LTV target between 20% to 25%, followed by fleet renewal, and then shareholder returns. While the leverage target is still far away as newbuilds deliveries increase the leverage, the company continues its steady buybacks of around $10 million per quarter. These repurchases are highly accretive and contribute to long term value creation as Navios trades considerably below its NAV.

This quarter the company also increased its quarterly dividend by 20%, to $0.06 per share. Given the low dividend, I consider this to be mainly testimonial and without impact.

Financial Position and Valuation

Navios has a strong financial position with $411 million in cash and $2.2 billion in debt, resulting in a net LTV around 30%. In 2022 this ratio was at 45% and the fleet was older. Moreover, as tanker and drybulk vessels values are increasing, the LTV will be further reduced which could compensate some of the debt increase when the newbuild delivers.

Q4 earnings exceeded analysts’ estimates as I anticipated in my last article, and I expect a similar outcome with Q1 results. However, Q1 results are more difficult to estimate because the company provided full year figures:

Despite this, I believe Q1 will be similar to Q4. Q4 2025 realized rate was $25,500 per day and contracted revenue is already above that. During Q1 the tanker indexed rates should provide a nice tailwind as rates are being outstanding. On the other hand, drybulk will be a drag as usually Q1 is the softer month, but this year rates have been astonishingly strong and just slightly below Q4 rates. This is why I believe the company will achieve at least an EPS of $3, considerably above analysts’ estimates:

Regarding valuation, NAV per share has increased since Q3 report, moving from $137.8 per share to $140.3 at the end of Q4.

Given the strong cash generation, the last few weeks asset prices increases and the repurchases, I expect Q1 NAV to be above Q4 2024. Despite the stock moving almost 40% YTD, it still trades at just 0.5x times NAV.

Management has demonstrated its commitment to repurchase after more than one year with a steady pace, and it recently increased its testimonial dividend. I expect they will increase its repurchase pace if the stock still trades at a meaningful discount to NAV and leverage approaches its target.

In my last article I valued the company at 0.6x times NAV; however, given the consistent capital allocation, and the company great prospects I am adjusting it to 0.7x times, giving a fair value of $100 per share. This represents about a 40% upside.

Risks

The main risks remain largely unchanged. The rate volatility, particularly in the drybulk segment, continues to be the main factor affecting earnings. While this exposure provides upside in strong markets, it will also negatively impact earnings if rates decline.

The second main risk is the capital allocation. While I expect some newbuilds, if the company starts a large newbuild program I believe it will not be well received by the market. Alternatively, if the company stops buybacks, it will negatively affect management perception and the discount will get bigger, impacting the valuation.

Conclusion

Despite Navios stock rising by more than 80% in the last year, it continues to be an interesting buy.

This overperformance is mainly due to the change in perception regarding management. If management continues its steady course, the stock should continue to close the valuation gap against peers. However, to move closer to or above NAV, the capital allocation should shift to higher dividends in the future.

data.title: APA Corp: Redefined By Operational Efficiencies And Middle East Turmoil
data.description:
Thesis

APA (APA) has reinvented itself in just a short 12 months. Almost exactly a year ago, I wrote a piece on the company to discuss its poor performance, generating just $841m in free cash flow. However, thanks to an aggressive cost improvement program, the company generated over $1B in 2025 free cash flow.

This performance was generated amidst a year-long decline in crude oil prices, giving credence to the company’s efforts. APA has not-so-simply, reinvented itself as a top tier operator.

The company has also assembled more ammunition through its gas development program in both the Permian and Egypt. The company has the optionality to access strong international pricing through improved Waha pricing, LNG export marketing, and even the Egyptian government.

Thanks to these profitability efforts, APA still trades at solid free cash flow yields. This value serves as a nice complement to the long-term potential of the company’s Suriname project. This project is expected to increase the company’s free cash flow by 50% in 2029, making APA an easy BUY recommendation.

Earnings Review

APA produced another strong quarter, generating $425m in free cash flow. The strong sequential performance was aided by a generally light quarter of capital spend.

For the full year, APA was able to generate $1B in FCF, despite declining oil prices over the period. This is a 19% improvement over 2024 levels and is a strong testament to the value creation efforts that APA has conducted over the course of the year.

During 2025, the company made significant strides in cost control. As shown below, APA has reduced its cost profile by $300 million, while holding total production volumes flat y-o-y. This is a very significant milestone as it accounts for roughly 30% of the company's 2025 free cash flow generation.

The bulk of these savings were expected to be derived by reducing the capital intensity of the company's drilling program. Thus far the company has been fairly successful in this endeavor, driving total well costs down by mid-single digits in the Permian.

This results in a net improvement of $300m in annual drilling and completion capital. By making each well more capital efficient, the company is also able to pull in economic inventory to refresh its development program.

APA now estimates that it holds over 1,000 well locations that generate greater than a 10% return at $50 WTI. At current activity levels, this translates into nearly 8 years of highly profitable activity. This figure expands to 1,500 wells and 11 years of profitable activity at $60/barrel, making for a rather robust inventory set.

More importantly, APA made a conscience effort to delineate that innovation is still alive and well in this industry. That same innovation continues to refresh the backlog of economical and technically available projects.

The most prominent development of this earnings season (across multiple operators) is the test of the deeper Barnett shale play. APA will be testing this zone with a four well package as it works toward driving this interval from “technical upside” to its growing economic inventory package.

This type of innovation is giving the Permian staying power despite the “peak shale” narrative. In particular, using this approach APA’s technical team has allowed the company’s inventory replenishment to exceed full year production. The company improved reserves by nearly 9% y-o-y, an impressive improvement by any standard.

Significant Natural Gas Leverage

APA also has an underappreciated natural gas portfolio. This stems from an operational setback years ago at its Alpine High acreage. This acreage is in one of the gassier benches of the Permian and has struggled to remain productive in recent years due to extremely low Waha hub pricing.

However, 2026 looks to be an inflection point for Waha as over 4 BCF/d of long-haul pipeline capacity comes online. While this capacity is back weighted toward the end of 2026, the improvement in the forward index is obvious. As a result, Permian gas should yield significantly more value for producers in 2027.

The value for APA is significant on a portfolio that generates 0.5 BCF/d. In 2025, the company reported a realized price of just $1.02/MCF. Boosting this value to even $2/MCF increases the company's free cash flow by $175m. This creates a straightforward path to double digit free cash flow growth in 2027.

The company has also had massive success on its gas development program in Egypt. In early 2025, the company was able to broker a deal with the Egyptian government that gave APA access to premium natural gas pricing.

The company is expecting a 45% price improvement over 2024 levels, thereby driving the returns on natural gas drilling to parity with oil development. As a result, nearly 50% of the company's Egyptian development will be focused on liquids rich gas development. For the full year, APA expects to realize $4.25/MCF, creating a win-win for both parties.

Due to the higher prices, the company expects to increase natural gas production by approximately 15% in 2026. However, recent developments could push that production even higher. Following the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a significant portion of global LNG has been removed from the market.

This development could help APA in two ways. The most obvious would be the incentive to deliver more gas to the Egyptian market. Secondarily, the LNG spot price is expected to increase significantly if these cargoes are held out of the market for any significant period of time. This will allow the company's natural gas market division to extract even higher premiums on its Waha priced gas.

Prior to the conflict, APA expected to generate $650m in pre-tax cash flow from its marketing division. This is accomplished through a combination of LNG contracts with Cheniere (LNG) and leveraging the differential between the Waha hub and the US gulf coast.

Now that LNG may be in short supply, we can expect the global LNG spread to widen. In turn, this should increase the price of gas at Henry hub, widening the differential to Waha. With roughly 0.5 BCF/d of unhedged volume exposure, this dynamic could again be very significant for APA's near-term free cash flows.

Valuation

I'll start this discussion by looking at EV:EBITDA, and then shift my focus to Price to FCF. As shown in the chart below, APA continues to trade at a significant discount to its peers who are focused solely on North American operations.

By trading at just three times EBITDA, APA falls squarely in value territory. In my opinion, the discount here is far too wide just for the sole fact that APA has assets in Egypt and the North Sea. I see this as a very loose reason to pay 60% more for every dollar of EBITDA generation from APA's peers.

While EBITDA is an important metric, the conversion of EBITDA to FCF is potentially even more important. In this area we can see a similar discount to Devon Energy (DVN) and Ovintiv (OVV). Investors are paying a 50% premium on every dollar of free cash flow generated out of these firms over APA.

Both of these distinct data sets show that APA trades at a significant discount to its peers. Should it trade at a discount because of its foreign exposure? Perhaps. But by trading at pennies on the dollar to its peers, I have a strong conviction that APA is undervalued.

This argument becomes more convincing when factoring in the long-term potential of the Suriname project. Typically, the market pays up for the potential of such a large growth project ($500m in FCF growth by 2029). However, we are not seeing any premium for APA's shares, implying deep value compared to the broader market.

Risks

Geopolitical factors are the most influential topic in the current oil market. Massive market disruptions are underway that have the potential to shift the market from oversupplied to undersupplied almost overnight.

Oil has surged from the low-$60 range, to the upper $70s in just a few weeks (CL1:COM). Now that the Strait of Hormuz has been closed by the Iranian government, time is the most influential factor. The longer the world is forced to operate without a major oil artery, the larger the depletion on global inventories.

This could be a significant event for the industry. However, its impacts could be just as quick to dissipate as they arrived. A short resolution will unwind any geopolitical premium and allow trade flows to restore to normal. Such an event would likely allow crude and LNG prices to return to the early February levels causing a correction in the energy sector.

Investor Key Takeaways

1. APA increased free cash flow generation by 19% in 2025, despite lowering crude oil prices.

2. This performance was created by a strong effort on cost controls. As a side effect to these efforts, the company was able to pull in economic inventory, fully replacing 2025's production.

3. Deeper Permian benches and other non-core targets represent what APA deems "technical upside", giving APA a long runway of inventory.

4. APA's unique gas portfolio affords it multiple avenues to participate in the growing need for natural gas. Margin can be extracted at the well head, at the gulf coast, or in Egypt, giving the company multiple options to grow FCF even in a flat oil market.

5. APA continues to be undervalued compared to peers and future growth prospects do not appear to be appropriately priced into the stock price.

6. I continue to rate APA as a BUY. The closure of the Strait of Hormuz may significantly ramp APA's profitability.

data.title: Hewlett Packard Enterprise: Strong AI-Led Growth Setup
data.description:
Hewlett Packard Enterprise Company (HPE) is gearing up to report fiscal first quarter 2026 results on Monday, March 9, 2026, and the setup could not be better, in my opinion. The enterprise-focused server maker will likely publish a massive addition to its AI system backlog, and the expansion of the HPE GreenLake hybrid Cloud platform could provide vital impulses for investors to push shares of HPE into a new up-leg.

Given that CapEx spending has been exploding lately, and projections continue to be highly bullish in the Data Center market, the server company is on track to potentially report record results and materially add to its AI-driven revenue backlog. Shares of Hewlett Packard Enterprise are cheap, trading at just 7.9X forward earnings, which in my opinion translates into a risk profile that is highly skewed to the upside.

Previous rating

Hewlett Packard Enterprise is gearing up to report fiscal first quarter 2026 results on Monday, March 9, 2026, and the setup could not be better, in my opinion. The enterprise-focused server maker will likely publish a massive addition to its AI system backlog, and the expansion of the HPE GreenLake hybrid Cloud platform could provide vital impulses for investors to push shares of HPE into a new up-leg.

Given that CapEx spending has been exploding lately, and projections continue to be highly bullish in the Data Center market, the server company is on track to potentially report record results and materially add to its AI-driven revenue backlog. Shares of Hewlett Packard Enterprise are cheap, trading at just 7.9X forward earnings, which in my opinion translates into a risk profile that is highly skewed to the upside.

All eyes on HPE's AI-driven growth

Hewlett Packard Enterprise is expected to report $0.59 per-share in non-GAAP earnings for Q1 '26 next Monday which implies a year-over-year growth rate of 20%. The top line figure, as per Seeking Alpha's consensus estimates, sits at $9.35B, at the top end of HPE's quarterly revenue guidance ($9.0-9.4B). The estimate trend is also widely positive with analysts upgrading their predictions 11 times in the last 90 days (vs. just 1 EPS estimate down-grade).

I expect the hardware platform to deliver strong results for the first fiscal quarter, especially in its networking division, which saw 150% year-over-year growth in the previous quarter, mainly because of the $14.0B acquisition of Juniper Networks in July 2025. This acquisition pivoted HPE’s business direction from a server-heavy hardware provider to a dominant force in AI-native networking.

The networking market is seeing strong growth, driven by Data Center Networks and Security, which is set to support Hewlett Packard Enterprise's results next week. In addition to core networking segment performance, investors will also be looking forward whether or not HPE will be able to capture more synergies from the Juniper Networks acquisition as the enterprise previously guided for $600M in annualized synergies all the way through FY 2028.

Most importantly, the company has a good chance to make an impression with regards to its AI orders... which I previously indicated were surging: in Q4 '25, the company's total AI orders stood at $13.4B and they have marked consistently new successive highs in the last three years. For Q1 '26, HPE is likely going to continue to report a surging AI orders and a record AI order backlog.

While I expect the company to report revenue at the top end of guidance (~$9.4B), HPE will likely been able to post a sequential improvement in its gross margin amid an ongoing shift to higher margin Cloud services. While Dell (DELL) has the largest business in terms of total gross profits, HPE is actually the most profitable one, with a gross profit margin in excess of 30%.

The main reason for this is the acquisition of Juniper Networks and the HPE GreenLake edge-to-cloud platform which is attracting more enterprise users. In the last quarter, HPE had about 46k users of GreenLake and the company will likely have added at least a couple of hundred lucrative enterprise accounts to its platform in the last quarter, potentially allowing the company to report a sequential increase in its gross margin on Monday.

HPE's valuation implies high safety margin

Shares of Hewlett Packard Enterprise are currently priced at 7.9X forward earnings and have seen sideways stock movements so far this year. Dell already reported blowout earnings last week, so I believe HPE, which benefits from the same underlying growth drivers in the AI-optimized server market, is set for a strong earnings report card.

Dell’s results were driven by strong growth in its key Infrastructure Solutions Group and the enterprise saw a massive bump in its AI-driven server orders. Dell currently has a forward P/E ratio of 10.2X which makes it one of the most affordable hardware companies benefiting from AI-led spending in the Data Center market. For comparison, Super Micro Computer (SMCI) is trading at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 10.4X which translates to an industry group average P/E ratio for these three hardware giants of approximately 9.3X.

I believe that Hewlett-Packard Enterprise, just like Dell, is worthy of a higher earnings multiplier, especially as AI orders are taking off and there is no slowdown in hyperscaler-driven CapEx spending. This backdrop provides long term fundamental support for growth in the fastest-growing business for HPE.

Hewlett Packard Enterprise could be trading at a 15.0X P/E ratio long term, in my opinion, with the Q1 '26 earnings report next Monday potentially being a big catalyst. Dell's shares, for instance, soared 22% after the server maker's reported last quarter earnings a week ago, mainly due to accelerating AI-optimized server revenue growth. A ~15.0X P/E ratio for HPE calculates to a fair market value of Hewlett-Packard Enterprise's shares of $41/share (implying 90% upside potential).

Primary risks with HPE

The biggest risk for Hewlett-Packard Enterprise, in my opinion, would be slowing adoption momentum of the GreenLake Cloud platform which is where the company is seeing strong growth momentum and which drives the company’s results in the important Hybrid Cloud segment. What would get me to downgrade shares of Hewlett Packard Enterprise is if the company saw a slowdown in AI order growth or if it failed to expand its gross margins next week.

Final thoughts

Given that Dell reported blowout earnings for its server business last week -- which is part of its out-performing Infrastructure Solutions Group -- resulting in record revenue, it is highly likely that Hewlett Packard Enterprise will also submit an impressive earnings scorecard for Q1 ’26 next week.

I am looking forward to seeing sequential gross margin growth for Q1 '26, amid a shift towards higher margin Cloud services, strong customer acquisition for GreenLake and a confirmation of the firm's synergy potential resulting from the Juniper Networks acquisition.

Further, HPE shares are extremely cheap, and have upside potential if the company reports solid AI-driven revenue growth on Monday. I see a favorable investment setup ahead of the Q1 '26 earnings report and continue to recommend HPE as a Buy for investors with a long term investment horizon.

data.title: Caterpillar: Everyone Is Betting On AI Bottlenecks, There Is One Being Overlooked
data.description:
Investment Thesis

There are two major tailwinds to Caterpillar (NYSE: CAT), a legacy American construction and energy engineering equipment manufacturer.

  • The consistent and macro one is that the Company is poised to benefit from President Trump's domestic industrial policy, increase in homebuilding activities, as well as the United States' global oil production push - but these macros are already priced in (to be discussed in the valuation part) since Trump's term, so this is not the thesis we will elaborate on today.
  • The opportunistic tailwind is the "AI bottleneck play" that rotates upward of the supply chain—from - from GPUs components to AI data centers and, ultimately, electricity

Major AI bottleneck is energy

When AI infrastructure is being developed rapidly, we start to see "bottlenecks" that are limiting the Moore's Law being played out. Electricity is one of them. To simplify the problem of electricity in one sentence is:

Traditional model of building the data center first, connecting to the grid after, is no longer feasible

This is because the current grid infrastructure, which has been built for over a decade, can no longer handle the exponentially growing electricity demand, if not being destroyed. There are two major reasons:

Transmission is a larger problem than generation

The electricity demand of AI is constantly high and growing, and data centers run 24/7. However, the conventional electricity generation capacity is either being planned slowly or is "intermittent" which is the major characteristic of solar and wind. But in either case, they are "far from where the demands are", which makes transmission difficult and overloaded. If you see the below roadmap of conventional electricity generation, you will understand how many "lines" we need for the many gigawatt-hours of electricity we need.

You can think of the transmission problem as a highway built decades ago catering to the numbers of cars in modern society—traffic jams and depreciation happen very quickly. In fact, there are 2,300 gigawatts of total generation waiting from ~10,300 projects to be connected to the grid as of 2024. What's worse is that, in some states where AI data centers are being built, it requires 7 years of average waiting time before it can start.

To an AI company, this is like telling them to wait for 7 years before it can further scale up and realize the revenue. The cruel fact is Moore's Law of AI development can't wait that long.

Reliability requires a full-grid upgrade

To AI data centers, especially those critical hyperscalers, they require as much quantity as quality - hence reliability is an issue. Another stat in the US shows that the average system interruption duration in 2024 is around 10 hours per year, according to the EIA.

To a reliability-first data center, it cannot afford a 10-second outage, not to mention an average outage rate at the unit of hours. Therefore, hyperscalers are looking for more cost-efficient and reliable electricity generation methods that can substitute the old infrastructure, which is grid-based.

This is where investment opportunities lie.

On-site electricity: The Holy Grail of Computing Energy Source

When the grid is no longer sufficient, when the traditional central generation model is slow, and when long-range transmission is cost-inefficient, "on-site generation" solves the problem, and this is why companies like Bloom Energy forecast more than 25% of electricity supply to data centers to be "on-site".

Despite sacrificing the economy of scale of electricity generation (through large-scale, remote generation plants), it offers two incomparable advantages, which are "timeliness" (skipping the year-long planning and connection time) and "certainty" (unreliable grid). Therefore, this is why companies are chasing after the trend.

The Strength of Caterpillar

There is an investable angle for Caterpillar other than its typical "yellow machinery" impressions in homebuilding and mining—reciprocating engines / Generator.

In 2024, the company showed a pivotal business milestone: for the first time of in its history, revenue of the energy and transportation segment exceeded that of the construction machinery segment, at $28.9 billion. This is the reason why CEO Joseph Creed mentioned in its latest earnings that energy is the largest and fastest-growing segment, driven by strong demand for turbines in data centers. Caterpillar's technology backbone is its "turbine" used in the top industrial machinery. Extending its strength into a new, lucrative business application, which is AI, is the most reasonable business strategy.

Caterpillar, unlike scaled generation plants or other modular energy sources, has an agile approach in deploying machinery in remote areas where new AI data centers are being built. Delivery of these turbine generators is also faster than its competitors in "on-site generation" like Bloom Energy, which needs a long turnaround for its energy storage boxes.

Valuation: The market has not priced in such opportunities

One straightforward way of valuing an amateur-stage company is to calculate from its margin, growth, and reinvestment rate to determine the value of the business from its engines of profitability, efficiency, and risk. As of year-end 2025, its financial metrics are

TTM Revenue $67.59 billion
Net Profit Margin (TTM) 13.1%
ROE 45.1%
Cost of Equity 7 - 7.5%

Using the above parameters and reverse-engineer the implied growth rate from its current share price at around $700, the market is pricing in a growth rate at merely ~4.5%. With the AI sector and its associated energy demands at double-digit annual growth rate in the next decades, this shows a significant undervaluation on Caterpillar as it's still being assumed at a 4.5% growth.

PEG ratio also shows that Caterpillar is significantly more fairly valued than GEV, which is another AI energy bottleneck trade.

Investment Risks

  • AI bubble: Largest risk of the company is the burst of the AI bubble, when valuation of all AI-related companies has skyrocketed in a bull market. However, given the volatility causes by the broader market sentiment worrying about the realistic value of AI companies, Caterpillar will be affected once valuation is to be rationalize
  • Economy downturn: With regional wars, conflicts and sticky inflation looming, US economy still faces challenges which might see a downturn. As a heavy industrial cyclical stock, Caterpillar will be affected by the macro market economic environment
  • New technologies: The thesis for Caterpillar is based on the fact that is offers agile and economical solutions. However, the likes of small modular nuclear reactor and battery technologies may improve efficiency significantly, making Caterpillar's more traditional solution obsolete

data.title: Baron Growth Fund Q4 2025 Shareholder Letter
data.description:
Neal Rosenberg

Ron Baron

Portfolio Manager

CEO, Portfolio Manager

Dear Baron Growth Fund Shareholder,

Performance

Baron Growth Fund® (the Fund)(BGRFX) declined 2.69% (Institutional Shares) for the quarter ended December 31, 2025. This trailed the return of the Fund’s benchmark, the Russell 2000 Growth Index (the Benchmark), which gained 1.22% for the quarter. The Russell 3000 Index, which measures the performance of the 3,000 largest publicly traded U.S. companies, gained 2.40% for the quarter.

Our strategy of owning a portfolio of competitively advantaged small and medium-sized businesses remained out of favor for most of this quarter. We observed an improvement in early December, as investors showed some renewed enthusiasm for the high-quality stocks (as defined by MSCI Barra) that exclusively populate our Fund. We believe that the valuation of the portfolio is particularly compelling given the significant divergence between its consistent earnings growth and its recent performance.

The Fund endured an unusually challenging year. Since March 31, the Fund declined 10.37%, which compares to a 27.15% gain in the Benchmark. We attribute approximately 60% of our relative underperformance to several positions where the market’s assessment of AI risk differed from our own. We attribute most of the remaining relative underperformance to our focus on owning leading businesses in their sectors, which has been the foundation of our strategy since inception.

We believe that our practice of exclusively investing in businesses with superior characteristics has been the primary driver of the Fund’s favorable long-term results, which has exceeded the performance of the Benchmark by 354 basis points annually since inception. In 2025, this strategy was out of favor, as investors elected to sell higher-quality investments like those that we own to buy riskier stocks. The magnitude of this shift was historically notable. According to data from MSCI Barra, performance for the Earnings Quality factor during the nine-month period ended December 8, 2025 was in the 100th percentile since 1975, which is when data first became available. Conversely, the positive performance of the Beta and Residual Volatility for this period are in the 1st and 6th percentiles, respectively. The few instances where Beta and Residual Volatility have performed better occurred mostly during the dotcom and post-COVID rallies.

We also experienced declines in several stocks where the market’s assessment of AI’s impact differed from our own. This cohort, which includes stocks such as Gartner, Inc. (IT), CoStar Group, Inc. (CSGP), Clearwater Analytics Holdings, Inc. (CWAN), FactSet Research Systems Inc. (FDS), MSCI Inc. (MSCI), and Guidewire Software, Inc. (GWRE), presently represents approximately 42% of our portfolio. On average, these stocks declined 15% in 2025 despite producing 10% revenue growth and 15% EPS growth. This significant divergence between growth and stock performance represents valuation compression, primarily due to investor concerns that AI will disrupt these companies in the future. We remain optimistic that all boast vast total addressable markets (TAM), sustainable competitive advantages, and attractive and durable growth prospects. We expect their valuations to expand as these characteristics are again recognized by the market.

We were encouraged by the improvement in our relative performance in December, as our style started to come back into favor and the market began to re-evaluate some of the names that it had previously deemed AI losers. We observed a change in the market in early December following mixed earnings results from AI-high fliers Oracle and Broadcom. From December 8 through December 31, the Fund outperformed by 635 basis points, with the Fund’s style biases, specifically Earnings Quality, Beta, and Residual Volatility being positive drivers of results. The average return for the cohort of stocks in the portfolio that had been deemed AI-losers outperformed the market by approximately 7% over that period. While that condition has not sustained early in 2026, we believe it demonstrates the significant potential for outperformance when our style is in favor.

We believe that the recent performance of Clearwater exemplifies the impact of the “AI narrative” on stock prices and demonstrates the dramatic upside potential of these investments as the market rethinks its assumptions. Clearwater is a leading provider of investment accounting and analytics services delivered via cloud-native software. Clearwater serves a vast TAM that is at least 10 times larger than its current scale. Management has built a remarkable financial model, underpinned by 20% organic revenue growth, margins in the mid-30% range, robust free cash flow generation, and near-perfect retention rates. In late 2024, shares of Clearwater peaked around $33 per share, up almost 80% from its IPO price just three years before. In early 2025, the company announced acquisitions that further expanded its TAM, bolstered its product portfolio, and positioned it to sustain or accelerate its earnings growth over time. Despite bright long-term prospects, investors began to view Clearwater as a generic AI-loser. Shares declined consistently over the course of 2025, reaching a low below $16 in early November. This represented a 50% peak-to-trough decline in share price despite the company growing its EBITDA almost 33% organically and almost 70% when including acquisitions.

Despite the stock’s decline, we assessed the likelihood of portfolio accountants choosing to write their own software as minimal. Clearwater’s financial results continued to prove this out, and the stock began to rally off its lows, although the price remained significantly disconnected from our assessment of fair value. In December, a consortium of private equity firms announced a deal to acquire Clearwater for $8.4 billion, which we view as a high-conviction refutation of the AI-loser narrative. The takeout is coming at a 47% premium to Clearwater’s pre-deal price and an approximately 55% premium to its November low, although we still believe that this price undervalues the business. We are optimistic that all our AI-impacted investments will develop similarly to Clearwater.

We believe that the portfolio is well positioned to generate compelling returns on a go-forward basis. Over time, we believe that the performance of stocks closely tracks the growth of the underlying businesses. In 2025, we estimate that the portfolio grew its earnings by 12% on average. However, the Fund declined 14% for the year, which implies that the portfolio’s aggregate valuation contracted by 26%. This is despite the Fund generating higher margins, cash flow, and returns on capital versus its historical composition and our Benchmark. We are optimistic that as the market’s appreciation for high quality businesses returns and its perspective on AI’s impact more closely aligns with our own, the Fund’s valuation will expand, enhancing the returns we already expect from consistent compounded earnings growth. We believe that the 635 basis points of outperformance generated in just three weeks demonstrates the significant pent-up potential currently embedded in the portfolio.

The Fund has gained 11.62% on an annualized basis since its inception on December 31, 1994, which exceeds that of the Benchmark by 3.54% and the Russell 3000 Index by 0.61%. We attribute this result to not losing money during periods of significant market drawdowns. While the Fund did not make much money from December 31, 1999, through December 31, 2008, a period which includes the highs of the Internet Bubble and the lows of the Financial Panic, it did generate a positive annualized return of 2.46%. This is approximately 2.7 times greater than the $111,073 the same hypothetical investment made in a fund designed to track the Benchmark would be worth, and 19% more than a hypothetical investment in the Russell 3000 Index. Hypothetically, our returns were achieved with approximately 29% less volatility than the Benchmark, as represented by its beta. (Please see the Performance in Challenging Times and Performance Characteristics tables.) FIGS, Inc. designs and sells scrubwear for health care professionals through a digitally native, direct-to-consumer strategy. Shares rose after the company reported quarterly results that beat expectations and raised its outlook for revenue and profits for the remainder of the year. FIGS’ revenue grew 8% due to robust customer demand for its health care apparel, supported by improving execution and normalizing industry trends. Demand for health care apparel remains largely non-discretionary and replenishment driven. The company also delivered stronger-than-expected profitability, benefiting from meaningful operating leverage while continuing to invest in the business. In addition, FIGS continues to expand its three key growth initiatives: international markets, TEAMS (its enterprise and group ordering business), and retail. Internationally, the company refined its growth strategy and is targeting 60 planned markets by year-end, up from 33. Within TEAMS, FIGS continues to add talent and develop new technology solutions to support growth. In retail, the company plans to open three stores (New York, Houston, and Chicago) with further expansion anticipated into 2026.

Shares of specialty insurer Arch Capital Group Ltd. rose on strong earnings results and active capital management. Third-quarter earnings per share beat Street expectations due to improved underwriting margins and very low catastrophe losses, as there were no landfall hurricanes in the U.S. this season for the first time since 2015. Return on equity of 18% exceeded management’s long-term target, driving 9% growth in book value per share, or 18% growth when adjusted for a special dividend. In addition, a faster pace of share repurchases reduced the share count by 4% year to date, signaling management’s confidence in the company’s valuation. We continue to own the stock due to Arch’s strong management team and our expectation for continued growth in earnings and book value over time.

Veterinary diagnostics leader IDEXX Laboratories, Inc. contributed to performance after again reporting better-than-expected financial results. Foot traffic to veterinary clinics in the U.S. remains modestly negative but is poised to recover over the next several years. Even so, IDEXX’s excellent execution has enabled the company to continue delivering robust performance. We believe IDEXX’s competitive trends are outstanding, and we expect new proprietary innovations—such as InVue, MultiCue, and CancerDX— to be meaningful contributors to growth in the years ahead. We also see increasing evidence that long-term secular trends around pet ownership and pet care spending have structurally accelerated, which should help support IDEXX’s long-term growth rate.

CoStar Group, Inc. is the leading provider of information and marketing services to the commercial and residential real estate industries. Shares fell as the company’s net new sales came in below expectations. The stock has been weighed down by significant growth investment in CoStar’s residential product, where sales performance has remained modest. That said, we are encouraged by improving momentum as the company builds out its dedicated residential sales force, enhances its customer targeting, and potentially benefits from changes in Multiple Listing Service practices. We also expect growth in CoStar’s non- residential business to accelerate as sales productivity ramps and the sales team refocuses on core offerings, a trend likely to be amplified by 20% sales force growth in 2025 alone. We believe the value of CoStar’s core non-residential business exceeds the current share price of the stock, suggesting that investors are ascribing little value to the long-term residential opportunity.

Shares of specialty insurer Kinsale Capital Group, Inc. fell during the quarter due to concerns about moderating growth amid a cyclical slowdown for the property and casualty insurance industry. While third-quarter revenue growth improved sequentially, the pace of improvement was more modest than suggested by monthly data from state insurance commissioners. Nevertheless, Kinsale reported quarterly earnings that exceeded Street expectations, driven by higher earned premiums, very low catastrophe losses, and favorable reserve development. We continue to own the stock because we believe Kinsale is well managed and has a long runway for growth in an attractive segment of the insurance market.

Choice Hotels International, Inc. is a global franchisor of economy and midscale hotels across a portfolio of well-known brands. Shares declined amid investor concerns over continued revenue per available room (RevPAR) weakness at the company’s lower-end brands. However, Choice continues to grow through unit expansion, albeit at a slightly slower pace, by adding larger and more upscale hotels that generate higher RevPAR and carry higher royalty rates, helping offset near-term pressure. Developers are also accelerating new franchise signings with Choice, indicating that the company’s brands continue to resonate. In addition, Choice is managing costs effectively and generating strong cash flow, which it is using to support increased dividend payments, share repurchases, and potential tuck-in acquisitions. The company also maintains a strong balance sheet, with financial leverage below its targeted levels, providing additional flexibility to accelerate share repurchases at current valuation levels.

Portfolio Structure and Investment Strategy

We seek to invest in businesses with attractive fundamental characteristics and long-term growth prospects. These attributes include high barriers to entry, sustainable competitive advantages, large and growing addressable markets, and durable secular tailwinds. We invest in business models that have recurring or predictable revenue, generate attractive incremental margins, are cash generative, and are not dependent on third-party financing. We invest with management teams that seek to consistently reinvest into their businesses to raise barriers to entry and pursue long-term profitable growth. We work with our growing team of analysts to conduct iterative and holistic due diligence by interacting with representatives of all company stakeholders. In addition to visiting regularly with a company’s management team, we join our analysts in speaking with a company’s existing and potential customers, key suppliers, and large competitors. We use such findings to refine our understanding of a business and its industry, assess its growth trajectory, test the durability of its competitive advantages, and ultimately reinforce or refute our investment thesis. We do this in an iterative manner and ultimately spend as much time researching long-held positions as we do when researching new potential investments.

We hold investments for the long term. As of December 31, 2025, our weighted average holding period was 18.6 years. This is dramatically longer than most other small-cap growth funds, which, according to Morningstar, turn over about 72% of their portfolios annually based on an average for the last three years. The Fund’s portfolio is designed to significantly outperform over the long term. Accordingly, it is much different than other funds that align more closely to index compositions. The portfolio’s 10 largest positions have a weighted average holding period of 19.7 years, ranging from an 9.1-year investment in Kinsale Capital Group, Inc. to an investment in Choice Hotels International, Inc. that exceeds 29 years. We have held 12 investments, representing 85.9% of the Fund’s net assets, for more than 10 years. We have held seven investments, representing 18.7% of the Fund’s net assets, for fewer than 10 years. The portfolio manager defines “Quality” as well-managed, competitively advantaged, faster growing companies with higher margins and returns on invested capital and lower leverage that are leaders in their respective markets. Note that this statement represents the manager’s opinion and is not based on a third-party ranking. Beta measures a fund’s sensitivity to market movements. The beta of the market (Russell 2000 Growth Index) is 1.00 by definition. MSCI Barra’s Earnings Quality factor explains stock return differences due to uncertainty around company operating fundamentals (sales, earnings, cash flows) and the accrual components of their earnings. Positive exposure indicates low balance sheet and cash flow accruals, and low variability of company fundamentals. Negative exposure indicates high accruals and high variability of fundamentals. Free Cash Flow (FCF) represents the cash that a company generates after accounting for cash outflows to support operations and maintain its capital assets. Free Cash Flow (FCF) Margin is a measure of profitability for a business. FCF Margin takes the free cash flow that a business generates and compares it against

the revenue they earned during the same period. Operating Margin is a company's profit for every dollar of sales after deducting production costs like wages and raw materials but before accounting for interest and taxes. The Residual Volatility factor captures the volatility of the stock specific return component of a security. The stock specific return component tries to describe the idiosyncratic behavior of a company’s stock price movements that is not attributable to other factors in the Barra risk model. Positive exposure to this factor indicates high stock specific volatility, while negative exposure indicates low stock specific volatility. Return(s) on invested capital (ROIC) is a calculation used to determine how well a company allocates its capital to profitable projects or investments.
